# Program flow {.unnumbered} ::: {.callout-tip} ### How does our code know what order to do things in? ::: ::::: {.columns} :::: {.column width="30%"} ```{mermaid} graph TD; A(operation-1) --> B(operation-2); A(operation-1) --> C(operation-3); B(operation-2) --> C(operation-3); C(operation-3) --> D(operation-4); D(operation-4) --> E(operation-5); C(operation-3) --> E(operation-5); style A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style B fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style C fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style E fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px ``` :::: :::: {.column width="30%"} ```{mermaid} graph TD; A(operation-1) --> B(operation-2); B(operation-2) --> C(operation-3); C(operation-3) --> D(operation-4); D(operation-4) --> E(operation-5); style A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style B fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style C fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style E fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px ``` :::: :::: {.column width="30%"} ```{mermaid} graph TD; A(operation-1) --> B(operation-2); A(operation-1) --> C(operation-3); B(operation-2) --> C(operation-3); A(operation-1) --> E(operation-5); C(operation-3) --> D(operation-4); D(operation-4) --> E(operation-5); D(operation-4) --> C(operation-3); style A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px style B fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style C fill:#ffffff,stroke:#4088d4,stroke-width:4px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style D fill:#ffffff,stroke:#4088d4,stroke-width:4px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style E fill:#ffffff,stroke:#FF6F59,stroke-width:4px ``` :::: ::::: ## Mechanisms to control program flow In python, program flow can be altered in four main ways: 1. **With conditional statements** that act as a branching mechanism. 2. **Using repetitive loops** through the same block of code. 3. **Using custom functions (subroutines)** to jump to an entirely different spot, even in a different file or module, to do a task before (usually) jumping back again. 4. **Through catching errors** and executing different chunks of code accordingly. To use these control mechanisms, we first need a way to tell python how our code is grouped together. ## Indentation Indentation, or leading whitespace, is extremely important in Python. Where other languages use specific ```begin``` and ```end``` statements or curly braces ```{}```, python uses indentation to identify which code is part of discrete blocks. :::{.fragment} ```yaml Statement_1: Command_A # statement 1 block Command_B # statement 1 block Statement_2: # statement 1 block Command_C # statement 2 block Command_D # statement 2 block Command_E # statement 1 block Command_F # outside all the blocks ``` ::: :::{.fragment} :::{.callout-note} ### The style guide, PEP 8, recommends spaces instead of tabs for indentation. ::: ::: ## Conditional control ### The *if* statement {.nostretch} In the real world, we often evaluate information to choose a course of action: ![](images/if-this.png){fig-align="center" width=45%}

**If the sun is shining, then I’ll go to the beach.**

In Python, the ```if``` statement is how we perform this sort of decision making. The most basic ```if``` statement has the format: ```python if expr: # evaluated as a boolean statement # valid python operation which is indented ``` So, our beach example might look like: ```python if sun_shining: go_beach = True ``` But what if we *aren't* going to the beach - how do we decide what to do instead? ```python if sun_shining: go_beach = True else: # This will be the default option go_shopping = True ``` We can also offer an alternative using ```elif```: ```python if sun_shining: go_beach = True elif snowing: go_skiing = True else: # This will be the default option go_shopping = True ``` We can even offer multiple alternatives using ```elif```: ```python if sun_shining: go_beach = True elif snowing: go_skiing = True elif raining: stay_home = True else: # This will be the default option go_shopping = True ``` ::: {.callout-note} ### *else* and *elif* blocks are optional, but if used must contain indented code ::: Source: [RealPython](https://realpython.com/python-conditional-statements/) ### Operators What about if we want to connect our decision to the *temperature* instead? Python has a standard collection of comparison (or relational) operators: ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} # is less than temperature = 11 print(temperature < 10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` False ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # is greater than temperate = 11 print(temperature > 10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` True ``` ::: ::: We can even combine comparisons to check if the temperature is in a range: ::: {.cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} temperature = 7 if temperature > 0 and temperature < 10: print("Go to the beach! 🏖") elif temperature < -10 or temperature > 40: print("Stay home") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Go to the beach! 🏖 ``` ::: ::: |Operator|Description|Example|Outcome| |-|-|-|-| |```<```|less than|```1 < 2```|True| |```>```|greater then|```1 > 2```|False| |```<=```|equal or less than|```2 <= 2```|True| |```>=```|equal or greater than|```1 >= 1```|True| |```==```|equality|```1 == 2```|False| |```!=```|non equality|```1 != 2```|True| |```is```|objects are identical| | | |```is not```|objects not identical| | | ::: {.cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} today = 500 tomorrow = 500 print(today == tomorrow) # True print(today is tomorrow) # False ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` True False ``` ::: ::: All objects can be assigned a status of "truthfulness"; generally empty containers are considered to be ```False```: | False value | Description | |-------------|-------------| | `None` | numeric equality | | `False` | False boolean | | `0` | 0 integer | | `0.0` | 0.0 floating point | | `""` | empty string | | `()` | empty tuple | | `[]` | empty list | | `{}` | empty dictonary | | `set()` | empty set | All others evaluate to ```True```. ## In practice ::: {.callout-caution} ### Exercises 2.4 Conditional control ::: ## Repetitive operations ### The *for* loop In the real world, we often need to repeat an action: ![](images/ball-p+o.png){fig-align="center" width=55%}

**Put five balls in the bucket**

In Python, the ```for``` loop allows us to perform this type of iterative repetition. A ```for``` loop executes commands in that block once for each value in a collection: ```python for item in collection: # item takes on each value in the collection statement # valid python operation that is indented ``` So, to put each ball into the bucket: ```python toys = ['ball1', 'ball2', 'ball3', 'ball4', 'ball5'] for ball in toys: bucket.append(ball) ``` ::: {.cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` The bucket contains: ['ball1'] The bucket contains: ['ball1', 'ball2'] The bucket contains: ['ball1', 'ball2', 'ball3'] The bucket contains: ['ball1', 'ball2', 'ball3', 'ball4'] The bucket contains: ['ball1', 'ball2', 'ball3', 'ball4', 'ball5'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ``` ::: ::: The same process works with any collection: ::: {.cell output-location='fragment' execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} toys = { 'ball1': 'orange', 'ball2': 'purple', 'ball3': 'orange', 'ball4': 'purple', 'ball5': 'orange'} for ball in toys.keys(): bucket.append(ball) ``` ::: What if we only want to collect the orange balls? ::: {.cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} toys = { 'ball1': 'orange', 'ball2': 'purple', 'ball3': 'orange', 'ball4': 'purple', 'ball5': 'orange'} for ball, colour in toys.items(): if colour == 'orange': bucket.append(ball) ``` ::: ::: {.callout-important} ### Loop variables can be called anything, but beware! Their state persists after the loop is finished. ::: ### Modifying flow What if we want to avoid one specific colour? ![](images/ball-coloured.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

**Avoid the *green* balls**

Along with ```if```, the ```continue``` and ```break``` statements allow us to modify which parts of the block are applied to the item. ```continue``` moves the internal counter directly to the next iteration without applying any code from lower in the block, and ```break``` stops the iteration immediately. What if we want to avoid one specific colour? ![](images/ball-coloured.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

**Avoid the *green* balls**

::: {.cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} toys = [ 'ball1-orange', 'ball2-pink', 'ball3-green', 'ball4-blue', 'ball5-purple'] bucket = [] for ball in toys: if 'green' in ball: continue bucket.append(ball) print(f'The bucket contains: {bucket}') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` The bucket contains: ['ball1-orange', 'ball2-pink', 'ball4-blue', 'ball5-purple'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} toys = [ 'ball1-orange', 'ball2-pink', 'ball3-green', 'ball4-blue', 'ball5-purple'] bucket = [] for ball in toys: if 'green' in ball: break bucket.append(ball) print(f'The bucket contains: {bucket}') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` The bucket contains: ['ball1-orange', 'ball2-pink'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ### What is happening here? ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} toys = [ 'ball1-orange', 'ball2-pink', 'ball3-green', 'ball4-blue', 'ball5-purple'] bucket = [] for ball in toys: toys.remove(ball) bucket.append(ball) print(f'The toys are: {toys}') print(f'The bucket contains: {bucket}') ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` The toys are: ['ball2-pink', 'ball4-blue'] The bucket contains: ['ball1-orange', 'ball3-green', 'ball5-purple'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-important} ### An internal counter is used to keep track of which item is used next, and this is incremented on each iteration. If you modify the collection while iterating, you will get some **interesting** bugs! ::: ### Built-in iterators The ```range()``` function allows you to iterate over a numeric sequence. ::: {.cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} my_list = [] for x in range(8): print("This round x is equal to:", x) my_list.append(x*x) my_list ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` This round x is equal to: 0 This round x is equal to: 1 This round x is equal to: 2 This round x is equal to: 3 This round x is equal to: 4 This round x is equal to: 5 This round x is equal to: 6 This round x is equal to: 7 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49] ``` ::: ::: Given a collection, ```enumerate()``` generates a tuple containing each value and it's index. ::: {.cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} letters = ['A','C','G'] for index, letter in enumerate(letters): print(index, letter) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0 A 1 C 2 G ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} numbered_letters = list(enumerate(letters)) print(numbered_letters) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [(0, 'A'), (1, 'C'), (2, 'G')] ``` ::: ::: ## In practice ::: {.callout-caution} ### Exercises 2.5 Repetitive operations :::
